one_line_takeaway: Reasoning ability in LLMs can emerge from pure reinforcement learning against verifiable rewards, without supervised reasoning demonstrations — DeepSeek-R1-Zero jumps 15.6%→77.9% on AIME, and the multi-stage DeepSeek-R1 reaches frontier reasoning benchmarks.
key_finding: Pure RL alone drives a large capability jump: DeepSeek-R1-Zero's AIME 2024 pass@1 rises from 15.6% to 77.9% over training (86.7% with cons@16 self-consistency), surpassing average human AIME participants, with response length growing organically as the model 'learns to think longer.' The final DeepSeek-R1 reaches frontier reasoning scores: AIME 2024 79.8 pass@1, MATH-500 97.3, CNMO 2024 78.8, Codeforces rating 2029 (96.3 percentile), LiveCodeBench 65.9, SWE-Bench Verified 49.2, MMLU 90.8, MMLU-Pro 84.0, GPQA Diamond 71.5 (Table 3, R1 column). The multi-stage pipeline mainly improves general/instruction benchmarks over the reasoning-only R1-Zero — e.g. final RL lifts AlpacaEval 2.0 by ~25% and ArenaHard by ~17%, and IF-Eval climbs from 46.6 (R1-Zero) to 83.3 (R1) — while reasoning stays roughly flat, confirming reasoning gains came from earlier RL stages.
core_contribution: The paper introduces DeepSeek-R1-Zero and DeepSeek-R1, showing that advanced reasoning in LLMs can be incentivized through large-scale reinforcement learning. Its own contributions are: (1) demonstrating that pure RL applied directly to a pre-trained base model (DeepSeek-V3-Base), with NO supervised fine-tuning stage, causes sophisticated reasoning behaviors (self-reflection, verification, exploration of alternatives, an emergent 'aha moment') to arise organically — this is DeepSeek-R1-Zero; (2) a multi-stage training pipeline (cold-start long-CoT SFT → reasoning-oriented RL → rejection-sampling SFT on reasoning+non-reasoning data → a final RL stage) that yields DeepSeek-R1, fixing R1-Zero's readability and language-mixing problems while retaining reasoning ability and aligning to human preferences; (3) distillation of this reasoning capability into smaller open models, released publicly on HuggingFace. Note: the RL algorithm GRPO (Shao et al., 2024) and the PPO baseline (Schulman et al., 2017) are adopted/compared, NOT invented by this paper.
limitations: Stated by the authors: (1) suboptimal structured output and no tool use (search/calculator); (2) token inefficiency / 'overthinking' on simple queries; (3) language mixing — optimized for Chinese and English, degrades on other languages; (4) prompt sensitivity — few-shot prompting consistently HURTS, zero-shot is recommended; (5) limited gains on software-engineering benchmarks because long eval times prevented large-scale RL there; (6) reward hacking — pure RL depends on reliable reward signals, which are hard to build for open-ended tasks like writing, so model-based rewards were used only for a few hundred final steps. Section 5's ethics statement notes R1 remains vulnerable to jailbreak attacks and to safety-compromising fine-tuning, with inherent safety rated 'moderate' (comparable to GPT-4o). Reviewer caveat: distillation results and cross-model comparisons live in supplementary sections (lines 1003+) not read in full, so distillation is described qualitatively only.

quantitative_results: R1-Zero AIME 2024 pass@1: 15.6% -> 77.9% during RL training; 86.7% with cons@16 self-consistency (surpasses average human participants)
Final DeepSeek-R1: AIME 2024 79.8 pass@1, MATH-500 97.3, CNMO 2024 78.8
R1 Codeforces rating 2029, percentile 96.3; LiveCodeBench 65.9 pass@1-CoT; SWE-Bench Verified 49.2 resolved; Aider-Polyglot 53.3
R1 general: MMLU 90.8, MMLU-Redux 92.9, MMLU-Pro 84.0, GPQA Diamond 71.5, IF-Eval 83.3, AlpacaEval 2.0 87.6 LC-winrate, ArenaHard 92.3
Final RL stage improved AlpacaEval 2.0 by ~25% and ArenaHard by ~17% with marginal reasoning change
R1-Zero trained ~10,400 steps (~1.6 epochs), batch size 512, 16 samples/question
Reward-model data: 66,000 helpfulness preference pairs; 106,000 safety-annotated prompts
Safety level rated moderate, comparable to GPT-4o (2024-05-13)
baselines_compared: PPO vs GRPO (on MATH with DeepSeek-Coder-V2-Lite)
